front: Under the Fourth Amendment, what are the TWO ways a person can be "seized"?
back: (1) The application of physical force to the body with intent to restrain; or (2) a show of authority to which the person submits.

front: In the seizure-of-the-person analysis, what question does the "seizure" inquiry answer, and what does it NOT answer?
back: It fixes only WHEN a seizure has occurred. Whether the seizure was reasonable (supported by reasonable suspicion, probable cause, etc.) is a separate question taken up later.

front: State the *Mendenhall* "free to leave" test for a Fourth Amendment seizure (verbatim).
back: A person is "seized" only if, "in view of all of the circumstances surrounding the incident, a reasonable person would have believed that he was not free to leave."

front: *Mendenhall*: a person is seized only if, in view of all the circumstances, a reasonable person would have believed that he was {{c1::not free to leave}}.
back: not free to leave

front: What kind of inquiry is the *Mendenhall* "free to leave" test — subjective or objective, and over what?
back: Objective and totality-based: it asks, in view of ALL the circumstances surrounding the incident, what a reasonable person would have believed — not the suspect's or officer's subjective state of mind.

front: *Mendenhall* listed circumstances that MIGHT indicate a seizure even if the person did not try to leave. Name them.
back: The threatening presence of several officers; the display of a weapon by an officer; some physical touching of the citizen; or the use of language or tone of voice indicating that compliance with the officer's request might be compelled.
cite: United States v. Mendenhall, 446 U.S. at 554

front: Which SCOTUS case supplies the objective "a reasonable person would not have believed himself free to leave" benchmark for show-of-authority seizures?
back: *United States v. Mendenhall*, 446 U.S. 544 (1980).

front: Under *Hodari D.*, when is a SHOW-OF-AUTHORITY seizure complete?
back: Only when the suspect actually submits (yields) to the assertion of authority. A suspect who flees an uncomplied-with show of authority is not yet seized.

front: *Hodari D.* answered the narrow question of whether, for a show of authority, a seizure occurs even though the subject does not yield. How did the Court hold?
back: It does not. With respect to a show of authority, no seizure occurs unless and until the subject yields/submits.
cite: California v. Hodari D., 499 U.S. at 626

front: Under *Hodari D.*, is a police command such as "Stop, in the name of the law!" shouted at a fleeing suspect a seizure?
back: No — a show of authority directed at a fleeing suspect "is no seizure" until the suspect actually submits to it.
cite: California v. Hodari D., 499 U.S. at 626

front: A suspect flees an uncomplied-with police show of authority and tosses cocaine while still running, before any officer reaches or touches him. Which case governs whether that contraband is the fruit of a seizure, and what is the result?
back: *California v. Hodari D.* — because he had not yet submitted, he was not seized, so contraband discarded mid-flight was not the fruit of a seizure and is not suppressible on that basis.

front: For a show-of-authority seizure, what is the relationship between the *Mendenhall* "free to leave" test and the *Hodari D.* submission requirement?
back: *Mendenhall*'s "free to leave" condition is necessary but NOT sufficient: it establishes that a show of authority existed, but the suspect must also actually submit before a seizure occurs.
cite: California v. Hodari D., 499 U.S. at 626

front: State the holding of *Torres v. Madrid* on physical-force seizures (verbatim).
back: "[T]he application of physical force to the body of a person with intent to restrain is a seizure even if the person does not submit and is not subdued."

front: Under *Torres v. Madrid*, at what precise moment does a physical-force seizure occur?
back: At the moment force is applied to the body with intent to restrain — even if the person never submits and escapes.

front: Officers shot a fleeing woman, but she kept going and drove away, never subdued. Which case holds she was nonetheless seized, and when?
back: *Torres v. Madrid* — she was seized at the instant the bullets struck her (force applied with intent to restrain), even though she was not subdued and drove off.

front: Under *Torres*, how is "intent to restrain" judged, and what kind of force does NOT count?
back: It is judged objectively — whether the challenged conduct objectively manifests an intent to restrain. Force applied by accident or for some other purpose does not count.

front: Under *Torres*, how long does a seizure by force last when the suspect does not submit?
back: Only as long as the application of force lasts — there is no "continuing arrest during the period of fugitivity." A suspect grazed by an officer's grasp who breaks free is seized for that instant only.

front: DISTINCTION: How do the submission requirements differ between a SHOW-OF-AUTHORITY seizure (*Hodari D.*) and a PHYSICAL-FORCE seizure (*Torres*)?
back: Show of authority (*Hodari D.*) requires the suspect to actually submit before any seizure occurs. Physical force (*Torres*) needs NO submission — the seizure is complete the moment force is applied with intent to restrain, even if the person is never subdued.
cite: California v. Hodari D., 499 U.S. at 626; Torres v. Madrid, 592 U.S. 306 (2021)

front: DISTINCTION: What gap left open by *Hodari D.* did *Torres v. Madrid* fill?
back: *Hodari D.* addressed show-of-authority seizures (requiring submission); *Torres* filled the force-without-submission gap, holding that applying physical force with intent to restrain is a seizure even when the suspect does not submit and is not subdued.

front: Why must the physical-force branch and the show-of-authority branch of seizure be analyzed separately?
back: They have different requirements: do not import the submission requirement into a force case, and do not import the force requirement into a show-of-authority case. "An arrest requires either physical force ... or, where that is absent, submission to the assertion of authority."
cite: California v. Hodari D., 499 U.S. at 626

front: Once a seizure by physical force has occurred, under what standard is its REASONABLENESS judged?
back: Under the objective-reasonableness standard of *Graham v. Connor*, 490 U.S. 386 (1989). Seizure is the trigger; *Graham* reasonableness is the next question.

front: PITFALL: An officer yells "stop" at a fleeing suspect who keeps running. Is the suspect already "seized"?
back: No. Until the suspect submits (or is touched with intent to restrain), there is no show-of-authority seizure — so anything discarded mid-flight is fair game. (*Hodari D.*)

front: PITFALL: How does a shot that HITS but fails to stop a suspect differ from a shot that MISSES, for seizure purposes?
back: A shot that hits but does not stop the suspect IS a seizure at that instant (*Torres*). A shot that misses applies no force to the body and is NOT a seizure.

front: PITFALL: Why is it wrong to treat *Mendenhall*'s "free to leave" inquiry as the whole seizure test?
back: For show-of-authority, "free to leave" is only the threshold — submission is still required (*Hodari D.*). For physical force, *Mendenhall* is beside the point — application of force with intent to restrain controls (*Torres*).

front: PITFALL: Why is "seized" not the same as "lawfully seized"?
back: Establishing that a seizure occurred does not make it reasonable. Whether reasonable suspicion, probable cause, or some other justification supported it is a separate analysis.

front: PITFALL: Does "intent to restrain" under *Torres* mean the officer's secret/subjective motive?
back: No. It is an objective inquiry into what the conduct manifests, not the officer's or suspect's subjective state of mind. (*Torres*)

front: An officer has only a HUNCH (no articulable facts). What authority to seize does the hunch grant, and what is the lawful tool instead?
back: A hunch grants NO authority to seize anyone. The lawful tool is a CONSENSUAL ENCOUNTER: the person remains free to leave, so no seizure occurs and no justification is needed. Properly articulated into specific facts, a hunch may rise to reasonable, articulable suspicion — the *Terry v. Ohio* predicate for an investigative detention (covered later).

front: PITFALL: May an officer treat a bare hunch as authority to detain or frisk a person?
back: No. Without articulable suspicion there is no authority to seize. Escalate via a consensual encounter (person free to leave) and build the articulation — do not detain or frisk on a hunch.
